facts.context: What is the structure of the United Nations Security Council?
facts.truth: 5 permanent members with veto power, 10 rotating members with no veto power
distractors: 5 permanent members and 10 rotating members, all with veto power
10 permanent members with veto power, and 5 rotating members without veto power
15 permanent members with veto power

facts.context: The Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty was the first accord
facts.truth: mandating the elimination of many long-range nuclear missiles.
distractors: on nuclear weapons signed between the United States and the Soviet Union.
cutting conventional arms in Europe.
to be rejected by the U.S. Senate.

facts.context: In American government, the power to declare war rests with
facts.truth: Congress.
distractors: the president of the United States.
the secretary of defense.
the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

facts.context: How did the Cold War context shape US perceptions of the Third World?
facts.truth: Local developments were viewed through a geopolitical lens
distractors: The US ignored the Third World
The US unreservedly supported decolonization
None of the above

facts.context: What, according to Systemic theories, is the primary determinant of a state's foreign policy?
facts.truth: The distribution of power in the international system
distractors: The character of a state's leader
The distribution of power within a state's governmental system
A state's political ideology

facts.context: What did the Moscow Treaty on Strategic Offensive Reductions do?
facts.truth: Required the United States and Russia to dismantle a significant number of their nuclear weapons
distractors: Required the United States and Russia to demobilize their armies
Prohibited the acquisition of new military bases
Prohibited short-range nuclear weapons

facts.context: Why did George H.W. Bush allow Saddam Hussein to remain in power after the Gulf War of 1991?
facts.truth: Limited UN mandate and fear of a protracted conflict
distractors: Lack of US firepower
Concern over oil supplies
Difficult terrain and fear of civilian casualties

facts.context: The dominant course for foreign policy throughout most of American history can be categorized as
facts.truth: isolationism.
distractors: containment.
neoconservatism.
protectionism.

facts.context: What are the key elements of the Liberal approach to US foreign policy?
facts.truth: Promotion of Democracy, free-trade and international institutions
distractors: Alliances, diplomacy and protectionism
The balance of power, self-sufficiency and prudence
None of the above

facts.context: In general, how do conservatives and liberals differ when it comes to defense spending?
facts.truth: Conservatives advocate increases in defense spending; liberals are generally skeptical of increases in defense spending.
distractors: Conservatives are skeptical of increases in defense spending; liberals advocate increases in defense spending.
Conservatives favor social spending over defense spending; liberals favor defense spending over social spending.
Conservatives emphasize strong economies; liberals emphasize strong military readiness.

facts.context: Intergovernmental organizations SELDOM do which of the following?
facts.truth: Provide strong enforcement
distractors: Monitor parties
Provide fora for discussion
Reduce transaction costs for agreements

facts.context: What is direct diplomacy?
facts.truth: Members of Congress negotiating directly with foreign governments
distractors: Face-to-face meetings between state leaders
The president consulting Congress on foreign policy issues
Bilateral talks that do not involve a third-party negotiator

facts.context: Which of the following considers it immoral to use force abroad to do good things (such human rights protection, democracy, etc)?
facts.truth: Realism
distractors: Idealism
Liberalism
None of the above

facts.context: According to realists, what is the fundamental difference between the international system and the domestic system?
facts.truth: Anarchy
distractors: Armed conflict
Institutions
No common language

facts.context: Why might the 'Philadelphian System' be linked to the idea of American exceptionalism?
facts.truth: It was designed as the antithesis of European politics
distractors: It encouraged greater involvement in European politics
It created a large standing army
It encouraged the centralization of political power in the US

facts.context: Détente, or the relaxing of tensions coupled with firm guarantees of mutual security, represented a shift in American foreign policy toward the communist world ushered in by
facts.truth: Richard Nixon.
distractors: Eugene McCarthy
Lyndon Johnson.
Robert Kennedy.

facts.context: Which of these is not a component of the 'American Creed'?
facts.truth: Autocracy
distractors: Liberty
Equality
Individualism

facts.context: According to International Institutionalists, what problems have international institutions been designed to address?
facts.truth: All of the above
distractors: War
Anarchy
Collective action and coordination problems

facts.context: The best known and perhaps most important international organization is
facts.truth: the United Nations.
distractors: the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO).
the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC).
the World Trade Organization (WTO).

facts.context: In what sense might exceptionalism link isolationist and internationalist strategies?
facts.truth: Both can be viewed as different means of achieving the same liberal ends
distractors: Both encourage world government
Both focus on the decline of the American power
It doesn't - the two are fundamentally opposed

facts.context: Why do realists tend to object to the influence of exceptionalism on American foreign policy?
facts.truth: It leads to a diminished focus on security, power and interest
distractors: It leads to a diminished focus on collective security
It diminishes the prospects for world government
It enhances the prospects for world government

facts.context: What did Paul Kennedy argue in his book The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers?
facts.truth: The United States could no longer remain a superpower and was in decline
distractors: All of the world's leading economies were declining due to low growth and inflation
The soft power of the United States would allow it to avoid decline
The rise of Japan had been exaggerated

facts.context: The Joint Chiefs of Staff is made up of
facts.truth: the commanding officers of each branch of the armed services, along with a chairperson and vice chairperson.
distractors: the critical domestic and foreign policy advisers to the president.
foreign policy advisers who meet with the president daily.
the highest-ranking generals in the U.S. Army.

facts.context: The cooperative international organization of 185 countries designed to stabilize the exchange of currencies and the world economy is
facts.truth: the International Monetary Fund.
distractors: the World Bank.
the United Nations.
UNICEF.

facts.context: What policies are part of a grand strategy of Offshore Balancing?
facts.truth: Embrace multi-polarity, show greater restraint internationally and require other states to meet their own security burdens.
distractors: Maintaining a balance between the United States and other countries and not entering into alliances
Investing equally in domestic and international security with balanced protection against terrorism and other states
Isolate the US from the international system and not seek global power

facts.context: What was a main organizational change proposed by the 9/11 Commission with regard to the U.S. intelligence community?
facts.truth: Creation of the National Intelligence Director
distractors: Creation of the Department of Homeland Security
Moving the Department of Defense Intelligence Agencies to the CIA for better coordination
Moving the domestic intelligence component of the FBI to the CIA

facts.context: Why might American exceptionalism lead to opposition to world government?
facts.truth: Its general anti-statism opposes centralized government
distractors: It views world government as impractical
It views the UN as a sufficient form of global governance
None of the above

facts.context: How did the relationship between President and Congress develop under George H.W. Bush and Bill Clinton?
facts.truth: None of the above
distractors: Congress asserted its primacy in foreign policy
US foreign policy became substantially decentralized
The Presidency welcomed the influence of Congress

facts.context: Historically, what has been the general pattern of defense spending in the United States?
facts.truth: Increased spending during wars, with demobilization bringing spending down but not back to prewar levels
distractors: Steady increases over time.
Unpredictable fluctuations.
Increased spending during wars, with further increases after the war to prepare for future engagements.

facts.context: What was 'democratic enlargement'?
facts.truth: Both b and c
distractors: A proposal for reform of the US system of government
A proposal for the extension of democratic rule globally
A proposal for the extension of free markets
